Understanding the internals of web & mobile helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with web & mobile has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying web & mobile in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Following best practices ensures effective use of Web & Mobile in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in Web & Mobile helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Optimizing Web & Mobile for production environments requires attention to key performance factors.
Implement request batching and connection pooling
Monitor token usage and optimize prompt length
Security is paramount when implementing Web & Mobile in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.

This section covered Web & Mobile in depth, including core concepts, practical implementation approaches, and production considerations. Apply these patterns consistently to build robust, maintainable systems.

Claude.ai Projects provide structured context persistence for development workflows. A Project stores a custom system prompt (Project Instructions), uploaded reference documents, and conversation history that persist across all conversations within that project. For developers, this eliminates re-explaining codebase conventions at the start of every session. Store your architecture decisions, code style guide, and key schemas in the project, and Claude will apply them automatically throughout your work.

# Using Claude.ai Projects for persistent development context

## Setting up a project for an ongoing codebase:
# 1. Go to claude.ai → New Project
# 2. In "Project Instructions" (the persistent system prompt), add:

---
This project is for Acme Backend API (Python/FastAPI).

Tech stack: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.115, PostgreSQL 16, SQLAlchemy 2.0
Testing: pytest with pytest-asyncio, 90%+ coverage required
Code style: Ruff formatter, type annotations on all public functions
Deploy: Kubernetes on AWS EKS, GitHub Actions for CI/CD

When writing code:
- Always add type hints
- Use async/await for all database operations
- Follow pattern in src/api/routes/ for new endpoints
- Check src/core/models.py before creating new DB models
---

# 3. Upload key reference files into the project:
#    - openapi.yaml (API schema)
#    - ARCHITECTURE.md (system design decisions)
#    - docs/conventions.md (team coding standards)

# 4. All conversations in this project start with this context automatically
#    No need to re-explain conventions in every chat session

Claude.ai's connector system uses OAuth 2.0 so remote MCP servers can act on behalf of authenticated users without those users sharing credentials with Claude directly. When you add a remote server URL in Settings > Connectors, Claude initiates an OAuth flow, the user authorizes access, and Claude stores the resulting token encrypted per-user. This is how enterprise integrations (Atlassian, GitHub, Notion) work in Claude.ai — your MCP server receives a Bearer token it can use to call upstream APIs on behalf of the authenticated user.

Claude.ai's web interface is a consumer product, not a public API. For programmatic access from applications, use the Anthropic Messages API at api.anthropic.com. The web interface and API share the same models but have different rate limits, pricing, and feature sets. Some features available in the web UI (Projects, Artifacts, remote MCP connectors) are not yet exposed through the API.

# Programmatic access to Claude: use the API, not claude.ai scraping
import anthropic

# CORRECT: Anthropic Messages API for programmatic access
client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=2048,
    system="You are a code reviewer for Python projects.",
    messages=[{
        "role": "user",
        "content": f"Review this function for security issues:\n\n{code_snippet}"
    }],
)
print(message.content[0].text)

# INCORRECT: Scraping claude.ai or automating the web interface
# - Against Terms of Service
# - Rate limits designed for human interaction speed
# - Breaks when UI changes
# - No access to model configuration or token counts

# What the API supports vs claude.ai web:
# API: tool use, vision, prompt caching, streaming, batches, model selection
# Web only (not yet in API): Projects, Artifacts, remote MCP connectors, voice

# For MCP in your application (use the SDK with local tool definitions):
client_with_tools = anthropic.Anthropic()
response = client_with_tools.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=[{  # inline tool definition
        "name": "run_query",
        "description": "Run a read-only SQL query on the production database",
        "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {
            "sql": {"type": "string", "description": "SELECT query to run"}
        }, "required": ["sql"]},
    }],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "How many users registered last week?"}],
)

Claude.ai web and Claude Code CLI have different security models that determine what tasks are appropriate for each. The CLI runs with your local OS user's permissions and can access any file or run any command that user can. The web interface runs in Claude's sandboxed cloud infrastructure and can only access what you explicitly provide — uploaded files, pasted content, or connected remote MCP servers.
: Full local filesystem, bash execution, local network — suitable for deep development work but requires trust in what you allow Claude to execute
: Sandboxed cloud environment — limited to what you provide, inherently safer for unfamiliar or untrusted tasks
: Private keys, credentials, and sensitive data sent to claude.ai transit Anthropic's infrastructure. Use CLI with local-only tools for secrets.
: CLI interactions log locally in ~/.claude/; web conversations stored per Anthropic's data retention policies
: Chat, Projects, Artifacts, remote MCP connectors, file upload, image analysis
: Local filesystem access, bash execution, local MCP servers, CLAUDE.md project memory, hooks and automation
: Voice input, camera capture, QR code scanning for Remote Control, push notifications for agent completion
: Full Artifacts rendering with JavaScript execution, Projects collaboration, connector management UI
: Use web/mobile for chat, content creation, and remote MCP tasks; use CLI for local file and code work
Common Pitfalls for Web and Mobile Usage
: Web interface rate limits are for human interaction speed. For bulk programmatic use, you need the Anthropic API with proper rate limit handling.
: Uploaded files in a regular conversation disappear next session. Use Projects to persist reference files across conversations.
: Mobile lacks inline diff views. For reviewing multi-file code changes, use web or desktop — the mobile experience is better for task submission than review.
: Remote Control connects to your local Claude Code session. The laptop must remain powered on and network-connected.
: Very large files uploaded to Projects can exhaust the context window. Split large codebases into focused reference documents.

#    - docs/conventions.md (team coding standards)

#    No need to re-explain conventions in every chat session

        "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {
            "sql": {"type": "string", "description": "SELECT query to run"}
        }, "required": ["sql"]},

    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "How many users registered last week?"}],
)
